subtitle: How the Prayaga Pillar Sang of Power and Song

panels.shot: low-angle epic wide of Samudragupta before a towering stone pillar
panels.action: Samudragupta stands in royal armor and diadem, one hand resting on a sheathed sword, the other lightly touching an ancient polished pillar rising above him amid the forts and trees of Prayaga on the Ganga-Yamuna confluence.

dialogue.text: In the age of the early Guptas, when old empires lay in shards, Samudragupta carved his hundred victories into stone and tuned his fame to the strings of a veena.

i18n.hi: प्रारंभिक गुप्त काल में, जब पुराने साम्राज्य बिखरे थे, समुद्रगुप्त ने सौ जीतें पत्थर पर लिखीं और वीणा की तारों पर अपनी कीर्ति बाँध दी।

panels.shot: bird's-eye wide over the Gangetic plain
panels.action: From high above, the fertile Gangetic plain stretches with winding rivers, scattered brick towns, and the modest yet rising city of Pataliputra where Samudragupta’s banner flutters over new Gupta halls.

dialogue.text: Two centuries after Ashoka, the north was a broken board of kings. Kushana riders, Nagas, Licchavis, and small lineages held fragments of Magadha’s old dream.

i18n.hi: अशोक के दो सौ साल बाद, उत्तर राह भटका राजपट्ट था। कुषाण, नाग, लिच्छवी और छोटी वंशियाँ मगध के सपने के टुकड़े पकड़े थे।

panels.shot: intimate interior mid-shot in palace hall
panels.action: A young yet resolute Samudragupta in simple but dignified court dress stands before elders and ministers in a modest Gupta hall, a map-board of the Gangetic lands laid out in front of them.

dialogue.text: Out of this patchwork rose the Guptas of Magadha, still small, still cautious. Their heir, Samudragupta, would inherit not a vast empire, but an opportunity.

i18n.hi: इसी उलझन से उठे मगध के गुप्त, न छोटे पर सतर्क। उनका वारिस समुद्रगुप्त, विरासत में साम्राज्य नहीं, मौका लेने वाला था।

panels.shot: medium close-up on Samudragupta in armor
panels.action: Samudragupta, now older, armored and crowned, grips the hilt of his sword, his gaze firm yet thoughtful, as behind him courtiers unroll palm-leaf records of rival kings and lineages.

dialogue.text: When he ascended around 335 CE, his enemies were many. But the young king saw in their rivalries a pattern—a mandala of power, with Magadha at its heart.

i18n.hi: लगभग ३३५ ई. में गद्दी पर बैठे तो दुश्मन ढेर थे। पर युवा राजा ने उनकी रंजिशों में एक मंडल देखा, जिसके बीचोंबीच था मगध।

panels.shot: dynamic low-angle battle wide
panels.action: On a dusty northern plain, Samudragupta rides at the head of armored cavalry in mid-charge, spears lowered, war elephants advancing behind, as distant enemy standards waver in the haze.

dialogue.text: The Prayaga inscription later boasted that he uprooted nine rulers of Aryavarta. Each name was a king of the north, each defeat a stone added to Gupta foundations.

i18n.hi: प्रयाग शिला बाद में फख्र से लिखती है कि उसने आर्यावर्त के नौ नरेश उखाड़ फेंके। हर नाम एक उत्तर राजा, हर हार गुप्त नींव की नई ईंट।

panels.shot: over-the-shoulder shot from Samudragupta on elephant
panels.action: From atop his decorated war-elephant, Samudragupta looks over a captured northern city with its gates open, local chiefs approaching with lowered heads and folded hands.

dialogue.text: These northern kings were not just cowed; their realms were absorbed. Aryavarta became the firm inner ring, ruled directly from Pataliputra’s courts and garrisons.

i18n.hi: इन उत्तरी राजाओं को बस डराया नहीं, निगल लिया गया। आर्यावर्त गढ़ बना, सीधा राजपट पाटलिपुत्र के दरबार और छावनियों से चला।

panels.shot: map-table top-down shot
panels.action: Inside a tent, Samudragupta leans over a painted board-map showing the Ganga valley and the route south, wooden tokens marking southern polities along the Dakshinapatha trade road.

dialogue.text: But power in India did not end at the Vindhyas. Beyond them ran the Dakshinapatha, the ancient southern road, lined with twelve proud kings who watched the north with wary eyes.

i18n.hi: पर शक्ति विन्ध्यों पर थमती नहीं थी। आगे थी दक्षिणापथ, पुरानी दक्षिणी राह, जहाँ बारह घमंडी राजा उत्तर पर चौकस नज़र रखे थे।

panels.shot: sweeping wide of army crossing Vindhyas
panels.action: A long Gupta column of infantry, cavalry and elephants winds along a forested mountain pass of the Vindhyas, torches flickering as they descend towards the plateau.

dialogue.text: Samudragupta drove his armies across the Vindhyas. Harishena, his poet, would later recite twelve southern names, conquered in a single southern arc of campaign.

i18n.hi: समुद्रगुप्त ने सेना को विन्ध्यों के पार धकेल दिया। कवि हरिषेण ने बाद में उन बारह दक्षिणी राजाओं के नाम गाए, जो एक ही दाक्षिण्य मोहीम में झुके।

panels.shot: mid-shot parley scene in southern court
panels.action: In a southern hall with distinct regional pillars and dress, a chained yet dignified southern king kneels before seated Samudragupta, who raises a hand in a composed gesture of release.

dialogue.text: But the Dakshinapatha kings were not uprooted like the northern. They were defeated, bound, then restored—captured, released, favored: graha, moksha, anugraha.

i18n.hi: पर दक्षिणापथ के राजा उत्तर वालों की तरह जड़ से नहीं काटे गए। उन्हें हराया, बाँधा, फिर छोड़ा—क़ैद, मुक्ति, अनुग्रह: ग्रह, मोक्ष, अनुग्रह।

panels.shot: medium close-up on Samudragupta in council
panels.action: Back in a council tent, Samudragupta sits with generals and Brahman advisers, weighing the costs as they gesture between models of distant southern forts and the heartland’s core.

dialogue.speaker: samudragupta
dialogue.text: Let the south keep its crowns—and remember who unbound them.

i18n.hi: दक्षिण को अपने मुकुट रहने दो—बस यह याद रहे, किसने उनकी बेड़ियाँ खोलीं।

panels.shot: wide of tributary kings offering gifts
panels.action: In an outdoor durbar, southern envoys in varied dress present elephants, gold and rare woods before Samudragupta’s raised throne, while scribes quietly record tribute on palm leaves.

dialogue.text: So the outer ring was shaped. Southern rulers kept their thrones, but sent gifts, daughters, and words of loyalty to Pataliputra. A web of tribute, not a grid of provinces.

i18n.hi: यूँ बाहर की परिधि बनी। दक्षिणी नरेश अपने सिंहासन पर रहे, पर उपहार, कन्याएँ और वफादारी के संदेश पाटलिपुत्र भेजते रहे। जाल था नज़रानों का, न कि सूबों की खूँटियों का।

panels.shot: medium wide of border with forest chiefs
panels.action: In a forested frontier clearing, Samudragupta meets rugged chiefs of jungle clans, their spears grounded, as they indicate their allegiance between the forests and the fields.

dialogue.text: Beyond even them lay forest peoples, frontier chiefs, and distant lords who sought his protection or feared his name. His realm grew as concentric circles, fading into influence rather than ending in walls.

i18n.hi: उनके पार थे वनजन, सीमा के सरदार, दूर देश के नाथ, जो या तो उनकी शरण चाहते थे या उनके नाम से काँपते थे। उनका राज्य गोल गोल फैलता गया, दीवारों से नहीं, असर से धुंधला होता।

panels.shot: slow push-in on Samudragupta in ritual attire
panels.action: Clad in ceremonial white with golden ornaments, Samudragupta stands beside a tethered, restless stallion under a sacrificial pavilion, Brahman priests preparing the ancient Vedic rite around them.

dialogue.text: Power demanded a language that others understood. Samudragupta revived the old Ashvamedha, the horse sacrifice not seen since the Kanvas, to proclaim sovereignty in priestly syllables.

i18n.hi: शक्ति को ऐसी भाषा चाहिए जो सब समझें। समुद्रगुप्त ने पुराना अश्वमेध जगाया, वह घोरा-यज्ञ जो कान्वों के बाद से सोया था, ताकि पुरोहितों की बोली में सत्ता का दावा कर सके।

panels.shot: symbolic wide of horse and guarded camp
panels.action: In a stylized scene, the sacred horse wanders under guard through countryside as armed attendants keep watch, villagers and small chieftains watching from a distance in awed submission.

dialogue.text: The ashva roamed, guarded, through lands he claimed. Where it walked unchallenged, priests would chant that no rival king dared deny his overlordship.

i18n.hi: रक्षा में घिरा अश्व उन भूखंडों पर दौड़ा जिन्हें वह अपना कहता था। जहाँ वह बेखटके चला, वहाँ ऋत्विज गाते थे कि कोई प्रतिद्वंदी उसकी अधिराज्यता को ललकार न सका।

panels.shot: extreme close-up of gold coin in hand
panels.action: A jeweler’s hand holds up a fresh gold coin, its gleaming surface showing Samudragupta standing by a sacrificial horse, with a faint impression of a standard and altar behind.

dialogue.text: Gold carried the tale farther than bards. On Ashvamedha coins, his figure stands beside the horse, frozen testimony that the old Vedic fires burned again under Gupta rule.

i18n.hi: सुनहरी मुद्राओं ने कथा को गायकों से दूर तक पहुँचा दिया। अश्वमेध सिक्कों पर वह घोड़े के पास खड़ा है, जमी हुई गवाही कि वैदिक अग्नि फिर गुप्त राज में भड़की।

panels.shot: intimate interior mid-shot of Samudragupta with veena
panels.action: In a quiet palace chamber lit by oil lamps, Samudragupta sits cross-legged in rich but relaxed robes, gently plucking a veena, eyes half-closed in concentration.

dialogue.text: Yet the same king who led horses to sacrifice also led notes across strings. His gold Lyrist coins showed him seated with veena, a Kaviraja, king among poets.

i18n.hi: पर वही राजा जो घोड़े यज्ञ को ले गया, सुरों को भी तारों पर ले चला। उसकी ‘लिरिस्ट’ स्वर्ण-मुद्राओं पर वह वीणा संग विराजमान है—कविराज, कवियों का राजा।

panels.shot: medium close-up profile with veena and sword nearby
panels.action: In profile, Samudragupta pauses mid-melody, the veena resting against his shoulder, while his sword and helmet lie within reach on a low table beside him.

dialogue.speaker: samudragupta
dialogue.text: Steel conquers bodies. Song travels where armies cannot.

i18n.hi: इस्पात शरीरों को जीतता है, गीत वहाँ पहुँचता है जहाँ फौज नहीं जा पाती।

panels.shot: grand interior wide of court with Harishena
panels.action: In the Gupta court, Samudragupta sits on a raised lion-throne, while court poet Harishena stands before him with palm-leaf manuscript, mid-recitation to assembled courtiers and envoys.

dialogue.text: His own compositions are lost to us, if they ever were written. But Harishena’s tongue became his trumpet, shaping a eulogy that would shine long after the voices fell silent.

i18n.hi: उसकी अपनी रचनाएँ, अगर कभी लिखी भी गईं, अब खो चुकी हैं। पर हरिषेण की ज़ुबान उसकी बिगुल बनी, ऐसी स्तुति गढ़ी जो आवाज़ों के थमने के बाद भी चमकती रही।

panels.shot: low-angle of Ashoka pillar being inscribed
panels.action: At Prayaga fort, artisans on scaffolding carve new Sanskrit lines into the polished surface of Ashoka’s ancient pillar, while Samudragupta and Harishena watch from below.

dialogue.text: They chose an old witness for new glory. On Ashoka’s pillar at Prayaga, Harishena etched in elegant script Samudragupta’s conquests, offerings, and virtues—a prashasti, a praise-song in stone.

i18n.hi: नई महिमा के लिए उन्होंने पुराने गवाह को चुना। अशोक के प्रयाग स्तंभ पर हरिषेण ने सुलेख में समुद्रगुप्त की विजय, दान और गुण उकेरे—प्रशस्ति, पत्थर में बँधा स्तुति-गीत।

panels.shot: close-up of inscription lines on pillar
panels.action: A detailed view of the pillar’s surface shows neat lines of Brahmi script, while in soft reflection Samudragupta’s figure shimmers faintly on the polished stone.

dialogue.text: It was not a survey but a sculpture of words. Nine kings uprooted, twelve humbled and freed, frontier peoples pacified, gifts from distant rulers—all arranged to magnify his image.

i18n.hi: वह सूखा वृत्तांत नहीं, शब्दों की मूर्ति थी। नौ राजा उखाड़े, बारह झुका कर छोड़े, सीमा जातियाँ शांत, दूर राजाओं के उपहार—सब यूँ सजा कि उसका प्रताप और ऊँचा दिखे।

panels.shot: scholarly tabletop shot with inscription and modern books
panels.action: In a time-bridging tableau, the pillar rubbing, a tray of Gupta gold coins, and modern scholars’ books lie on a table, hands of unseen researchers turning pages and examining the evidence.

dialogue.text: Later ages would call him the ‘Napoleon of India’, but that was a foreign mirror. His was no tight European bureaucracy—it was a layered mandala of might, gift, ritual and song.

i18n.hi: आने वाले युगों ने उसे ‘भारत का नेपोलियन’ कहा, पर वह पराई उपमा थी। उसका राज कोई यूरोपीय कसावट नहीं, परतों वाला मंडल था—बल, दान, यज्ञ और गीत का।

panels.shot: wide shot of Gupta city and school
panels.action: A bustling Gupta city scene shows merchants, temple builders, and a small group of students reciting before a teacher, while the royal standard flies in the distance over orderly streets.

dialogue.text: From this lattice of power grew what later poets would call a golden age. Not because wars ceased, but because power was used to steady roads, patronize learning, and make room for music.

i18n.hi: इसी शक्तियों के जाल से वह बना जिसे बाद के कवि सुर्णयुग कहेंगे। इसलिए नहीं कि युद्ध थमे थे, बल्कि इसलिए कि बल से राहें सँभाली गईं, विद्या पाली गई और संगीत को जगह मिली।

panels.shot: distant rear-view of Samudragupta walking away from pillar
panels.action: At dusk, Samudragupta’s figure is seen from behind as he walks slowly away from the towering Prayaga pillar, his silhouette framed against the meeting waters of Ganga and Yamuna.

dialogue.text: Stone cracks, coins are buried, and empires shift. But the memory of Samudragupta endures as that of a king who measured greatness not only in lands subdued, but in the culture his victories could shelter.

i18n.hi: पत्थर चिरते हैं, सिक्के मिट्टी में सो जाते हैं, साम्राज्य बदलते हैं। पर समुद्रगुप्त की स्मृति ऐसे राजा की है जिसने महानता को सिर्फ जीती ज़मीन से नहीं, उस संस्कृति से भी तोला जिसे उसकी जीतों ने पनाह दी।

subtitle_i18n.en: How the Prayaga Pillar Sang of Power and Song
subtitle_i18n.hi: प्रयाग स्तंभ ने गाया शक्ति और सुर का गीत
